title: Product BOM Decomposition: Türwächter IoT — Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools

sections.title: NAICS 611310 | Output Type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)
sections.content:
*Follows component spec: product-bom-decomposition.html (substeps B.1–B.4). Input: 01_product_decomposition.md, 02_functional_promise.md, 03_constraints.md, 05_market_discovery.md. Web search used for market share sourcing.*

---

sections.title: Routing Check
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS** | 611310 — Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools |
| **Sector** | Educational Services (61) — service output |
| **BOM applies?** | **YES** — BOM is of the *product sold into this market* (Türwächter IoT), not of the university. Physical device with full compositional structure. |
| **Output type** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard — IoT variant (Art. 990140) |
| **Applicable variant** | Exit Control 179 Pre-alarm + Radio (battery-operated, surface-mount, 868 MHz EasyWave) |
| **Key difference vs. K-12 (611110)** | Larger, more diverse building stock (labs, dormitories, lecture halls, research buildings). Stronger campus IT governance → protocol interop critical. Labs may require IP65+. Range constraint (C3) more acute in multi-storey campus buildings. |

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
| ID | Subsystem (L4) | Pattern category | University-context relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | **Mechanical Guard Subsystem** | Primary functional core | Blocks door handle; single-motion release per EN 179. Non-negotiable under C2 (power-independent). No difference from K-12. |
| S2 | **Acoustic Alarm Subsystem** | Primary functional (secondary) | 95 dB alert on activation. Mechanical trigger — zero power required. Fire code mandated across all campus buildings. |
| S3 | **Wireless Communication Subsystem** | Control / interface | Most strategically important subsystem in this market. Campus IT departments increasingly mandate open protocols (LoRaWAN, BACnet/IP). C6 (EasyWave lock-in) is the primary adoption barrier. |
| S4 | **Power Supply Subsystem** | Auxiliary / support | 9V battery. C4 (battery life) elevated in dormitory corridors and lecture hall exits — very high door-event frequency. Research building exits are lower frequency. |
| S5 | **Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem** | Containment / packaging | Surface-mounted housing. Research labs and wet labs may require IP65+ housing upgrade (C9). Standard ABS acceptable in administrative and lecture hall contexts. |

---

sections.title: B.3 — Alternatives at Every Level
sections.content:
### S1 — Mechanical Guard Subsystem

#### L3: Key Override Mechanism

| Alternative | Share | Trend | University relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Profile half-cylinder (EN 1303) | 55% | Stable | Standard; keyed to campus master-key suite; most common in legacy campus buildings |
| Cam lock (tubular) | 20% | Stable | Lower cost; adequate security in non-sensitive areas |
| Electronic / RFID key override | 20% | Growing | Audit trail aligns with campus access control policies; required in labs and controlled areas |
| No override (manual reset only) | 5% | Declining | Eliminates bypass risk; operationally impractical across large campuses |

**Confidence:** LOW | **Source:** Analyst estimate; no published split for door alarm override mechanisms. RFID share elevated vs. K-12 due to university access control maturity.

#### L1: Guard Arm / Lever Body Material → L0 Polymer

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABS (injection-molded) | 40% | Stable | Cost-effective; good impact absorption; indoor standard |
| PC/ABS blend | 35% | Growing | Flame-retardant grades (UL94 V-0); stronger; preferred where IK vandal ratings matter |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | 15% | Stable | Higher strength; heavier; weather-resistant |
| Aluminium alloy | 10% | Declining | Premium; high cost; rarely justified at this product level |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** PC/ABS market data (Credence Research 2024); [polycase.com material comparison](https://www.polycase.com)

---

### S2 — Acoustic Alarm Subsystem

#### L3: Sound Generation Module — Transducer Type

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric (active piezo element) | ~62% | Stable | Dominant. Low power draw; self-contained oscillator; operates without mains — only type compatible with C2. |
| Electromagnetic buzzer | ~28% | Declining | Coil-based; louder at low frequencies; higher current draw — less compatible with battery-primary constraint. |
| Speaker + class-D amplifier | ~10% | Growing | Configurable tones / voice alerts; significantly higher power; rare in battery-primary devices. |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Straits Research Buzzer Market 2024; Cognitive Market Research Electromagnetic Buzzers 2024. Piezo at ≥62% corroborated by two independent sources.

**University note:** Piezo is the only option compatible with C2 (mechanical alarm at 0 V). Speaker-based alternatives under evaluation for lecture-hall tone customization (different alert tones for fire vs. unauthorized exit), but require powered circuit; cannot replace mechanical trigger.

---

### S3 — Wireless Communication Subsystem

#### L3: RF Transmitter Module — Wireless Protocol

Most strategically significant BOM choice for the university market. C6 (EasyWave protocol lock-in) is the primary adoption barrier — campus IT departments increasingly mandate open-protocol IoT infrastructure. Universities investing in campus-wide LoRaWAN networks (common in EU research universities, US land-grant universities) cannot integrate proprietary sub-1GHz devices without a dedicated gateway.

| Alternative | Share | Trend | University fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proprietary sub-1GHz (433/868 MHz, e.g. EasyWave) | ~40% | Declining | Dominant in legacy alarm devices; no native BMS interop; requires GfS BUS gateway; procurement risk with IT governance. |
| LoRaWAN (868/915 MHz) | ~18% | Growing | Best fit for multi-building campus networks — penetrates 4–6 concrete floors; campus gateways amortize across entire IoT fleet; 41.1% CAGR (GMInsights 2025). |
| BLE 5.x (Bluetooth Mesh) | ~20% | Growing | Short range (~30m per hop); native mobile app access; well understood by campus IT; good for single-building retrofits. |
| Zigbee / Thread | ~13% | Stable | Mesh topology; used in campus building automation; requires coordinator hub; compatible with Matter standard. |
| Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) | ~6% | Stable | Easy IT integration; high power draw — battery life (C4) concern especially for dormitory exits. |
| NB-IoT / LTE-M | ~3% | Emerging | Carrier-grade reliability; per-device subscription cost; limited to high-security campus perimeter gates. |

**Confidence:** LOW | **Source:** Analyst estimate. LoRaWAN market context: GMInsights 2025 ($3.7B market, 41.1% CAGR). IoT Communication Protocol Market: $17.6B in 2024 (MarketGrowthReports). No published protocol split for door alarm devices specifically.

**University-specific context:** Campus Safety Magazine (2025) reports that 76% of campuses use electronic lock solutions; campus IoT infrastructure buildouts are accelerating. Universities with existing LoRaWAN deployments (growing in EU and US research universities) would require a LoRaWAN-capable module to avoid a separate EasyWave gateway per building.

#### L2: Guard-Position Sensor Type

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reed switch (magnetic-contact) | ~55% | Stable | Simple, reliable, zero power in idle state; dominant in cost-sensitive alarm devices |
| Hall effect sensor (solid-state) | ~30% | Growing | No moving parts; more vibration-resistant; preferred in high-traffic locations |
| Mechanical microswitch | ~12% | Declining | Reliable but wears over time; not ideal for high-frequency dormitory exits |
| Optical interrupt sensor | ~3% | Niche | High precision; high cost; used where debris contamination is a concern |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Analyst estimate based on broader IoT sensor market data

---

### S4 — Power Supply Subsystem

#### L1: 9V Block Battery — Electrochemical Technology (L0)

C4 (battery life floor) is highly variable across university building types. Dormitory corridor exits and lecture hall exits during class changes are high-frequency; research building and administrative exits are lower frequency. Specifying lithium primary for all dormitory-context deployments is strongly recommended.

| Alternative | Share (IoT door devices) | Trend | Battery life (high-use door) | University fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alkaline (e.g. Duracell MN1604) | ~52% | Stable | 12–18 months at moderate use | Acceptable for research / admin buildings; not recommended for dormitory exits |
| Lithium primary (e.g. Energizer L522) | ~31% | Growing | 24–36 months at same use | Strongly recommended for dormitory corridors and lecture hall exits; 47% of industrial IoT sensors now lithium primary (Straits Research 2024) |
| NiMH rechargeable (9V) | ~12% | Stable | Requires recharge every 6–12 months | Reduces waste; operationally intensive for facilities teams managing hundreds of campus doors |
| Proprietary rechargeable pack | ~5% | Emerging | Manufacturer-managed; USB-C charge | Emerging in premium IoT devices; not in current GfS product line |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Mordor Intelligence Primary Battery Market 2025; Straits Research IoT Battery Market 2024.

**University note:** At 9–35 mA draw (GfS spec), alkaline 9V cells (~550 mAh) may exhaust in under 12 months at high-frequency dormitory exits. Lithium 9V cells (~1,200 mAh) double service life and reduce maintenance callouts — essential given large campus door counts (a 500-door deployment requires 500 battery changes per cycle).

---

### S5 — Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem

#### L0: Housing Shell Polymer

| Alternative | Share (security device housings) | Trend | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABS (standard / FR grade) | ~40% | Stable | Cost-effective; UL94 V-0 grades available; adequate for administrative and lecture buildings |
| PC/ABS blend | ~35% | Growing | Best impact strength + flame retardance combination; preferred for IK07+ vandal ratings; recommended for dormitory corridors |
| Flame-retardant polycarbonate (PC) | ~15% | Stable | Higher temperature resistance; transparent-capable; used where LED indicator visibility matters |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | ~10% | Declining | Metal alternative; corrosion-resistant; specified in outdoor or wet-lab contexts where IP45 housing is insufficient |

**Confidence:** MEDIUM | **Source:** Credence Research PC/ABS Market 2024 ($4.67B); >58% of electronic enclosures require UL94 flame-retardant grades (Business Research Insights 2024).

**University note:** Dormitory corridors and student-accessible stairwells warrant IK07 impact rating — PC/ABS blend is the specification floor. Research wet labs and chemical labs require IP65 enclosure upgrades (C9); standard ABS and even IP45 variants are insufficient in washdown environments. Zinc alloy die-cast or IP65-rated polymer enclosures required.

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Mapping
sections.content:
### Component Category: Wireless Communication Module

Appears at one BOM position but drives the most consequential integration decision for campus deployments.

**Position:** S3 → L3 RF transmitter module → L2 RF PCB assembly → L1 RF transceiver IC

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Function | Detects guard-state change (guard opened / alarm triggered / battery low); transmits event to campus receiver or LoRaWAN gateway |
| Safety-critical | NO (supervisory only; C2 ensures mechanical alarm functions regardless) |
| University-specific issue | Campus IT departments increasingly require open-protocol IoT devices. EasyWave lock-in (C6) is a disqualifying criterion in RFPs from universities with existing LoRaWAN or BACnet/IP infrastructure. |
| Alternatives at this position | See S3 protocol table above. LoRaWAN and BLE are the growing challengers. |

**Insight:** The wireless module is the single BOM position where a component swap (EasyWave → LoRaWAN radio IC) would resolve the largest adoption barrier in the university market without altering any other BOM level. The mechanical, acoustic, power, and enclosure subsystems are protocol-agnostic. This is a targeted engineering change with outsized commercial impact.

---

sections.title: Market-Context Summary
sections.content:
| Constraint | Severity in 611310 | Affected BOM position | Mitigation path |
|---|---|---|---|
| C3 Wireless range | **HIGH** — large multi-storey campus buildings | S3 RF module | LoRaWAN (long range) or mesh repeater chain |
| C4 Battery life | **HIGH** in dormitories; LOW in research buildings | S4 battery chemistry | Lithium primary for high-traffic doors |
| C6 Protocol lock-in | **HIGH** — campus IT governance mandates open protocols | S3 RF module | LoRaWAN / BLE variant development |
| C8 GDPR | Medium | S3 data pipeline | Data minimization; DPA with monitoring SaaS |
| C9 IP class | Medium in wet labs | S5 enclosure housing | IP65 die-cast zinc or sealed PC/ABS variant |
| C5 Surface mount | Low — campus aesthetic tolerance higher than luxury hotels | S5 mounting hardware | Acceptable as-is for most buildings |

---

rows.Value: **YES** — BOM is of the *product sold into this market* (Türwächter IoT), not of the university. Physical device with full compositional structure.
Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard — IoT variant (Art. 990140)
Exit Control 179 Pre-alarm + Radio (battery-operated, surface-mount, 868 MHz EasyWave)

Larger, more diverse building stock (labs, dormitories, lecture halls, research buildings). Stronger campus IT governance → protocol interop critical. Labs may require IP65+. Range constraint (C3) more acute in multi-storey campus buildings.

rows.University-context relevance: Blocks door handle; single-motion release per EN 179. Non-negotiable under C2 (power-independent). No difference from K-12.
95 dB alert on activation. Mechanical trigger — zero power required. Fire code mandated across all campus buildings.
Most strategically important subsystem in this market. Campus IT departments increasingly mandate open protocols (LoRaWAN, BACnet/IP). C6 (EasyWave lock-in) is the primary adoption barrier.
9V battery. C4 (battery life) elevated in dormitory corridors and lecture hall exits — very high door-event frequency. Research building exits are lower frequency.
Surface-mounted housing. Research labs and wet labs may require IP65+ housing upgrade (C9). Standard ABS acceptable in administrative and lecture hall contexts.

rows.University relevance: Standard; keyed to campus master-key suite; most common in legacy campus buildings
Lower cost; adequate security in non-sensitive areas
Audit trail aligns with campus access control policies; required in labs and controlled areas
Eliminates bypass risk; operationally impractical across large campuses

rows.Notes: Cost-effective; good impact absorption; indoor standard
Flame-retardant grades (UL94 V-0); stronger; preferred where IK vandal ratings matter
Premium; high cost; rarely justified at this product level
Dominant. Low power draw; self-contained oscillator; operates without mains — only type compatible with C2.
Coil-based; louder at low frequencies; higher current draw — less compatible with battery-primary constraint.
Configurable tones / voice alerts; significantly higher power; rare in battery-primary devices.

rows.University fit: Dominant in legacy alarm devices; no native BMS interop; requires GfS BUS gateway; procurement risk with IT governance.
Best fit for multi-building campus networks — penetrates 4–6 concrete floors; campus gateways amortize across entire IoT fleet; 41.1% CAGR (GMInsights 2025).
Short range (~30m per hop); native mobile app access; well understood by campus IT; good for single-building retrofits.
Mesh topology; used in campus building automation; requires coordinator hub; compatible with Matter standard.
Easy IT integration; high power draw — battery life (C4) concern especially for dormitory exits.
Carrier-grade reliability; per-device subscription cost; limited to high-security campus perimeter gates.

rows.Notes: Simple, reliable, zero power in idle state; dominant in cost-sensitive alarm devices
No moving parts; more vibration-resistant; preferred in high-traffic locations
Reliable but wears over time; not ideal for high-frequency dormitory exits
High precision; high cost; used where debris contamination is a concern

rows.University fit: Acceptable for research / admin buildings; not recommended for dormitory exits
Strongly recommended for dormitory corridors and lecture hall exits; 47% of industrial IoT sensors now lithium primary (Straits Research 2024)
Reduces waste; operationally intensive for facilities teams managing hundreds of campus doors
Emerging in premium IoT devices; not in current GfS product line

rows.Notes: Cost-effective; UL94 V-0 grades available; adequate for administrative and lecture buildings
Best impact strength + flame retardance combination; preferred for IK07+ vandal ratings; recommended for dormitory corridors
Higher temperature resistance; transparent-capable; used where LED indicator visibility matters
Metal alternative; corrosion-resistant; specified in outdoor or wet-lab contexts where IP45 housing is insufficient

rows.Value: Detects guard-state change (guard opened / alarm triggered / battery low); transmits event to campus receiver or LoRaWAN gateway
NO (supervisory only; C2 ensures mechanical alarm functions regardless)
Campus IT departments increasingly require open-protocol IoT devices. EasyWave lock-in (C6) is a disqualifying criterion in RFPs from universities with existing LoRaWAN or BACnet/IP infrastructure.
See S3 protocol table above. LoRaWAN and BLE are the growing challengers.

rows.Mitigation path: LoRaWAN (long range) or mesh repeater chain

rows.Severity in 611310: **HIGH** in dormitories; LOW in research buildings
**HIGH** — campus IT governance mandates open protocols

rows.Mitigation path: Data minimization; DPA with monitoring SaaS
IP65 die-cast zinc or sealed PC/ABS variant

rows.Severity in 611310: Low — campus aesthetic tolerance higher than luxury hotels

entities.name: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard — IoT Variant
entities.description: Türwächter IoT (Art. 990140): surface-mounted, battery-operated emergency exit door alarm guard with 868 MHz wireless state reporting
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Mechanical Guard Subsystem
entities.description: Spring-loaded pivot mechanism that physically blocks the door handle and releases on 45° guard swing, simultaneously triggering the alarm via mechanical linkage. Operates without electrical power (satisfies C2).
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Acoustic Alarm Subsystem
entities.description: Piezoelectric alarm horn producing ≥95 dB at 1m. Mechanical trigger — no power required for base alarm function. Pre-alarm detection circuit present in 990140 variant.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Wireless Communication Subsystem
entities.description: 868 MHz EasyWave RF transmitter module with MCU and guard-position sensor. Detects and transmits guard-state events to receivers and BUS interfaces. Primary integration and protocol-compatibility concern for campus deployments.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Power Supply Subsystem
entities.description: 9V block battery (user-replaceable) with holder and voltage regulation circuit. Powers IoT radio module and pre-alarm circuit only; mechanical alarm is power-independent.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem
entities.description: Injection-molded housing (RAL 6029 safety green) plus rear backplate and handle-clamp mounting hardware. Surface-mount only. IP45 outdoor variant available; IP65+ required for wet-lab and washdown contexts.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Guard pivot mechanism
entities.description: Spring-return pivot assembly and handle-blocking element. Spring steel torsion spring + pivot pin + guard arm body.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Key override mechanism
entities.description: Profile half-cylinder lock assembly enabling authorized bypass without alarm trigger. Campus master-key compatible.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Sound generation module
entities.description: Piezoelectric buzzer assembly + resonance cavity. Mechanical trigger actuation — zero electrical power required.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: RF transmitter module
entities.description: 868 MHz EasyWave RF PCB assembly: RF transceiver IC, antenna, crystal oscillator, passive components. Protocol-locked — key architectural constraint for campus IoT integration.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Guard-position sensor
entities.description: Reed contact or Hall-effect sensor detecting guard-open state and transmitting event to MCU.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: 9V block battery
entities.description: User-replaceable 9V primary cell. Critical: battery chemistry selection (alkaline vs. lithium) determines service interval at high-frequency dormitory exits.
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)

entities.name: Proprietary sub-1GHz (EasyWave 868 MHz)
entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard (IoT Variant)
